serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of plum green tea is approximately 250 ml (250 g), equivalent to a regular takeaway cup commonly served at Singaporean bubble tea and beverage stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Plum Green Tea — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Plum Green Tea (cr_id D08010092) with confidence 0.95. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sugar-sweetened beverages and green tea analogues — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for plum-flavoured green tea. GI estimated at 55 based on analogous sugar-sweetened tea beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI 55 or below is classified as LOW (0–55 = low; 56–69 = medium; 70+ = high). Sucrose-sweetened beverages with similar sugar content (approximately 9–10 g/100 ml) typically yield GI values in the 50–60 range; 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low category. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as: (55 × 12.44 × 2.5) / 100 = 17.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.54 g/100 g) in a liquid beverage independently elevates blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sympathetic activation, despite very low sodium (1 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this sugar-sweetened beverage and choose unsweetened green tea instead, as the 9.54 g/100 g sugar load independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines regardless of the negligible sodium content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.54 g sugar/100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.03 g/100 g) causes rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation; liquid sugar is absorbed faster than equivalent solid food.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage entirely; the 9.54 g/100 g sugar in liquid form produces rapid glycaemic spikes, and the green tea polyphenols present in commercial preparations do not meaningfully offset this glycaemic impact — switch to unsweetened green tea to obtain catechin benefits without the sugar load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Regular consumption of sugar-sweetened beverages is associated with increased triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and elevated cardiovascular risk independent of total caloric intake.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit or eliminate this sugar-sweetened beverage from their diet, as habitual SSB consumption raises triglycerides and lowers HDL cholesterol; unsweetened green tea is a preferable alternative that provides cardioprotective catechins without the sugar burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar (9.54 g/100 g) promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity, offsetting any anti-inflammatory benefit from green tea catechins present in commercial preparations.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should choose unsweetened green tea to obtain genuine EGCG anti-inflammatory benefits, as the added sugar in this commercial plum green tea drink promotes inflammatory mediators and the catechin concentration is typically insufficient to provide meaningful clinical benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plum Green Tea (梅子绿茶) is a sweetened cold or iced beverage popular in Singapore, commonly sold at bubble tea shops, hawker centre drink stalls, and takeaway beverage counters. It is prepared by brewing green tea and combining it with plum flavouring — typically derived from preserved plum syrup or artificial plum essence — and sugar, resulting in a lightly tart, sweet, and refreshing drink. The beverage has a pale amber-green colour, a mild astringency from the green tea base, and a distinctive sweet-sour plum note. Per 100 g, it provides 48 kcal, 12.44 g carbohydrate, 9.54 g sugar, 0.01 g protein, 0.0 g fat, 0.03 g dietary fibre, and 1 mg sodium. The high sugar content (9.54 g/100 g) classifies this as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Despite the green tea base providing catechins (including EGCG) with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, the sugar load in commercial preparations typically negates these benefits. A standard serving is approximately 250 ml. Unsweetened green tea is recommended as a healthier alternative for individuals seeking the polyphenol benefits of green tea without the glycaemic burden.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Plum Green Tea (cr_id D08010092) with a reported confidence of 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are from this approved primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, appropriately documented.
notes: fat_g is confirmed zero (0.0) per HPB Singapore Foodo source — this is a sugar-sweetened tea beverage containing no fat by formulation; confirmed zero per HPB. saturated_fat_g is confirmed zero by the same rationale. protein_g is 0.01 g/100 g (trace level), not zero. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this commercial plum green tea beverage are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry as of 2026-04-16. GI value of 55 is an analogous estimate based on sugar-sweetened tea beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for plum green tea was found. Per the University of Sydney GI classification scale, GI 55 falls within the LOW category (0–55 = low; 56–69 = medium; 70+ = high); gi_category is therefore 'low'. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (55 × 12.44 × 2.5) / 100 = 17.1 g. Ontology codes use closest defensible approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry for plum-flavoured green tea exists as of 2026-04-16.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to match glycemic_index 55 per University of Sydney GI classification scale (0–55 = low). Updated gi_source notes and record notes to document this classification rationale explicitly.
